content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Every tool changes the shape of the human using it. This is not a metaphor. It is the oldest fact we have about ourselves.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The hand remembers the hammer.
Long before writing, before agriculture, before anything we would recognise as civilisation, our ancestors picked up stones and started knapping them into edges. Archaeologists can trace what happened next in our bones. The human hand, over hundreds of thousands of years of tool use, developed a longer thumb, broader fingertips, and a grip precise enough to thread a needle. The tool did not just extend the hand. It rebuilt it.
The same thing happened to the brain. Making a stone hand-axe requires planning several steps, holding a finished form in mind while working toward it, and correcting for mistakes. The regions of the brain that light up when a modern person knaps flint overlap with the regions involved in language. Some researchers believe toolmaking helped bootstrap the very capacity for complex thought. We did not simply invent tools. Tools, in a very real sense, invented us.
The media theorist John Culkin put it in a line so good it is usually misattributed to his friend Marshall McLuhan: “We shape our tools, and thereafter our tools shape us.”
That sentence is the whole history of technology in eleven words. The rest are examples.
Socrates saw it coming.
Around 370 BC, Plato recorded Socrates telling a story about the Egyptian god Theuth, inventor of writing, presenting his creation to the king. Writing, Theuth promised, would make people wiser and improve their memory. The king disagreed. This invention, he said, would do the opposite. People would stop exercising their memory because they could rely on marks on a page. They would have the appearance of wisdom without its substance, reciting things they had not truly internalised.
Socrates was not wrong. Oral cultures could hold entire epics in living memory. Homeric bards carried tens of thousands of lines in their heads. Within a few generations of widespread literacy, that capacity withered, because nobody needed it anymore. A skill nobody practices is a skill nobody has.
But Socrates was also not entirely right, and the way he was wrong matters. Writing did not merely replace memory. It created a new kind of mind. Externalising thought onto a page lets people examine their own ideas as objects, revise them, and compare them across centuries. Philosophy, law, mathematics, and science, as we know them, are what a literate mind can do that an oral mind cannot. We traded one capacity for another, and the trade reshaped everything.
Notice the irony: we only know Socrates’ warning about writing because someone wrote it down.
The clock got inside us.
The mechanical clock was not invented for commerce or industry. It was invented for prayer. Benedictine monks needed to ring the canonical hours, so medieval monasteries built machines to divide the day into regular, repeatable units. Then the machine escaped the monastery.
The historian Lewis Mumford argued that the clock, not the steam engine, was the defining machine of the industrial age, because it did something no engine could: it changed what time felt like. Before the clock, people ate when hungry, slept when tired, and worked with the light. Time was something that happened. After the clock, time became something you spent, saved, wasted, and ran out of. We synchronised our bodies to an abstraction. Hunger at noon because the clock says noon is not natural. It is manufactured, and it is now so deep in us that we experience it as instinct.
Henry David Thoreau, watching the railroads and telegraphs of his own century, wrote that “men have become the tools of their tools.” He was describing farmers enslaved to their equipment, but he could have been describing anyone who has ever felt their phone buzz in an empty pocket.
Nietzsche’s typewriter
In 1882, his eyesight failing, Friedrich Nietzsche bought a typewriter, a Malling-Hansen Writing Ball, so he could keep working with his eyes closed. A composer friend noticed that his prose changed on the new machine. It became tighter, more telegraphic, more aphoristic. Nietzsche agreed. “Our writing tools are also working on our thoughts,” he typed back.
That may be the most honest sentence ever written about technology. Not the tools we fear, not the tools we celebrate. The tools we barely notice, quietly working on our thoughts while we believe we are simply using them.
The modern evidence is blunt about this. In 2000, researchers at University College London scanned the brains of London taxi drivers, who at the time had to memorise the entire street map of the city, a feat called the Knowledge. Their posterior hippocampi, the brain region for spatial navigation, were measurably enlarged, and grew with years on the job. Two decades of GPS later, studies point the other way: people who habitually navigate by turn-by-turn instruction show less engagement in exactly those regions. The brain is a use-it-or-lose-it economy. Neuroplasticity does not care whether the change is one you would have chosen.
The internet did the same thing to memory that GPS did to navigation. A 2011 study led by Betsy Sparrow found that when people expect information to remain available online, they remember where to find it rather than the thing itself. Psychologists call this transactive memory, and it is not new; couples and teams have always divided remembering between them. What is new is that one partner in the transaction is now every fact ever recorded, available in your pocket. We remember less and look up more. Socrates, again, is half right.
The pattern under all of it
Line these stories up, and a pattern emerges that is more precise than “technology changes us.”
Every time we delegate a task, we stop practising the skill underneath it. Every time we stop practising, the skill fades, in the individual and eventually in the culture. And in exchange, if the trade is a good one, we gain capacity somewhere else: the literate mind, the coordinated society, the navigable globe.
The trade is never optional and never neutral. The only question is whether it is made deliberately or by default.
Which brings us to the tool currently working on.
What AI takes, and what it trains
Every previous tool automated something adjacent to thinking. The hammer took muscle. Writing took storage. The clock took timekeeping. The calculator took arithmetic. The search engine took a recall.
AI is the first tool that takes the drafting of thought itself. It writes the first version, proposes the plan, structures the argument, and suggests the decision. And here the historical pattern should stop us cold, because the skill we stop practising when we delegate thinking is not a peripheral skill. It is the one that all the other trades were supposed to be in service of.
Writing is the clearest case. Anyone who writes seriously knows that writing is not the transcription of a finished thought. It is how the thought gets finished. The blank page is where you discover that your idea has a hole in it, that two beliefs you hold contradict each other, that the thing you meant is not the thing you said. Delegate the drafting, and you can still get a document. What you do not get is the understanding that struggling through the draft would have produced. Cognitive scientists call this desirable difficulty: the effortful path is not an inefficiency in learning; it is the mechanism.
There is a second trap, and engineers have known about it for forty years. In 1983, the researcher Lisanne Bainbridge described what she called the ironies of automation: the more capable an automated system becomes, the less the human operator practices the skill, and the more critical that unpracticed skill becomes at the exact moment the automation fails or drifts. Aviation learned this the hard way, with pilots whose manual flying atrophied inside cockpits that rarely needed it. The irony compounds with AI, because the “failure” is not an alarm. It is a plausible, confident, fluent output that happens to be wrong, and the only defence is a human whose judgment is still sharp enough to catch it.
So the question is not whether AI will replace people. Mostly, it will not, in the same way writing did not replace memory so much as reorganise what memory is for. The question is what kind of people AI will produce. And there are two plausible answers.
One future produces operators who approve what they can no longer evaluate. People who clicked “accept” ten thousand times, whose judgment quietly atrophied inside a system that rarely needed it, until the day it did. This future does not arrive through malice. It arrives through convenience, one delegated decision at a time.
The other future produces people who are genuinely amplified. People who handled the machine for drafting and kept the judging. Who let AI multiply their output while deliberately keeping the core skill, the evaluating, deciding, taste-forming skill, in daily practice? The taxi driver’s hippocampus grew because the Knowledge made him do the navigating. The skill you keep is the skill the tool makes you use.
Which future we get is not fate. It is designed.
What we are doing about it at Relaystack
At Relaystack, we build AI agents that execute real work, and we think about this question constantly, because we are building one of the tools that will do the shaping. Here, we are thinking hard about how this tool will change you, and we are planning to handle that responsibly.
Our answer is a philosophy we call supervised autopilot. Our agents act autonomously, but they pause at consequential steps and put the decision in front of you. It would be easy to frame that pause as a safety feature, and it is one. But it is also something more deliberate: it is where your judgment stays in practice.
Every approval point is a moment where you evaluate, redirect, or veto. The agent does the hundred small motions; you make the calls that matter, and you keep making them, which means the muscle that makes them never goes quiet. We are not trying to build a tool you defer to. We are trying to build a tool you direct, one designed so that using it more makes your judgment sharper, not softer.
Nietzsche was right that our tools work on our thoughts. That is not something a tool can avoid doing. But a toolmaker can choose what the tool trains. The hammer shaped the hand, the clock shaped the day, and AI will shape the mind of everyone who uses it. The honest position is not to deny that. It is to design for it, and then to say so out loud.
Every tool changes the shape of the human using it. We would like the humans our tool produces to be sharper, more deliberate, and more in command than the ones who picked it up. That is the standard we are building against.
